title: Human vs. Metric Evaluation
concept: Metrics are fast but struggle with synonyms and style; human evaluation remains the gold standard for text quality. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: From RNNs to Attention.'

exercises.prompt: How do automatic metrics like BLEU calculate their scores?

options.text: Compare machine output against human reference translations.
Ask the machine if it feels confident about its work.
Count how many total pages the machine translated.

exercises.explain: Automatic metrics check how closely a machine's output matches a correct reference text.

cards.title: Who Judges Best?
cards.body:
We have learned about computer formulas that score translations in a split second. But can a simple math formula really tell if a poem was translated beautifully? 

Let's find out why computers sometimes struggle with the art of language, and why humans are still the ultimate judges!

cards.title: Human vs. Metric Evaluation
cards.body:
**Automatic metrics** (like BLEU or ROUGE) are computer programs that instantly check if the machine's translation matches a human's reference translation word-for-word. 

**Human evaluation** involves real people reading the translations to judge if they sound natural, convey the right meaning, and capture the correct style.

cards.title: The Baking Contest
cards.body:
Imagine you are a judge at a baking contest. 

A scale can instantly weigh a cake and tell you its size (like a computer metric). But to know if the cake actually tastes delicious, is moist, and looks beautiful, you need a human judge to take a bite. 

Computer metrics measure the size, but human judges evaluate the soul of the translation!

cards.title: Spotting the Synonyms
cards.body:
Suppose a machine translates a sentence as: *"The pup is joyful."*
The correct human reference is: *"The dog is happy."*

• **An automatic metric** might score this very low because none of the words match exactly.
• **A human evaluator** will give it a high score because "pup" means "dog" and "joyful" means "happy"!

cards.title: The Perfect Score Trap
cards.body:
**Myth:** If a translation gets a perfect score from a computer metric, it is guaranteed to be a perfect translation.
**Reality:** Not at all! A sentence can have all the right words scrambled in a weird order that makes no sense to a human, yet still score highly on basic computer metrics. Humans are always the final test!

cards.title: Side-by-Side Comparison
cards.body: Let's compare the strengths of computer-calculated metrics versus real human judges.

right.text: Slow and expensive, but understands flow, tone, and true meaning.

left.text: Super fast, free to run, but misses natural flow and synonyms.

exercises.prompt: What is the main advantage of using automatic metrics instead of human evaluators?

options.text: They are fast and inexpensive to run.
They are much better at understanding sarcasm than humans.
They always agree with human judges.
They can feel the emotion behind a translated poem.

exercises.explain: Automatic metrics are run by computers, making them incredibly fast and inexpensive to calculate compared to hiring human judges.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Human evaluation is considered the gold standard for measuring text quality because humans understand meaning and style.
exercises.explain: Human evaluation is slow and expensive, but it remains the gold standard because humans understand context, nuance, and flow better than any simple formula.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete this sentence about computer metrics:
exercises.sentence: Automatic metrics struggle to evaluate translations fairly because they often miss ______ that humans easily understand.
exercises.explain: Computer metrics look for exact word matches and often fail to recognize synonyms (different words with the same meaning).

exercises.prompt: Which of these translations sounds the most natural to a human evaluator?

options.text: The cat has run and run to the mouse house.
The clever cat chased the mouse right into its hole.
Cat smart did chase mouse inside home hole.

exercises.explain: Option B is written by a human. Option A is a repetitive machine translation failure. Option C is a word-for-word translation that misses the natural English idiom.

exercises.prompt: Let's review! What is the main trade-off when choosing between human evaluation and automatic metrics (like BLEU or ROUGE) for translation quality?

options.text: Computer metrics are always better because humans are too slow.
Metrics are fast and cheap, but human judgment is the ultimate gold standard for natural quality.
We should never use computer metrics because they are always wrong.
Metrics can easily understand humor, sarcasm, and style just like humans.

exercises.explain: Computer metrics are wonderful assistants for speed, but only humans can truly judge the beauty, tone, and correct meaning of a sentence! Next up: From RNNs to Attention.
